sentence_reviews.text: Benchmarks and task metrics define datasets, protocols, scoring rules, baselines, uncertainty, and reporting conventions for comparable evaluation.

sentence_reviews.text: Benchmarks, test suites, and task metrics make model behavior comparable across systems, versions, datasets, and risk controls.

sentence_reviews.text: A benchmark can overfit to public examples, miss deployment-specific harms, or reward a proxy that diverges from the intended behavior.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: NIST AI RMF 1.0, Map/Measure/Manage/Govern functions and AI RMF webpage overview on design, development, use, and evaluation of AI systems.

sentence_reviews.text: Benchmarks and task metrics use datasets, sampling, and train-test splits to define comparable evaluation cases.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: NIST AI RMF 1.0, Map/Measure/Manage/Govern functions and AI RMF webpage overview on design, development, use, and evaluation of AI systems.

sentence_reviews.text: Benchmark reports use statistics and experimental design to estimate performance, uncertainty, and comparisons.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: NIST AI RMF 1.0, Map/Measure/Manage/Govern functions and AI RMF webpage overview on design, development, use, and evaluation of AI systems.
sentence_reviews.notes: Narrowed from downstream graph positioning to benchmark/test evidence and risk-management evaluation language.

sentence_reviews.text: Many benchmarks depend on labels, targets, rewards, or preference judgments as scoring signals.
